title: Welcome to Docker
description: Welcome to the Docker Simulator! In this first level, you'll learn how to start a container. Docker containers are isolated environments that run applications.
objective: Start a container using the command: `docker run ubuntu`

title: Containerizing an Application
description: Let's package a real application! Imagine we have code for a Node.js Todo App in our current directory. We need to build a Docker image for it so we can run it anywhere.
objective: Build a Docker image named `todo-app` by running: `docker build -t todo-app .`

title: Running the Application
description: Now that our Todo App is packaged into a Docker image, let's run it! We'll use the `-d` flag to run it in the background (detached), the `-p` flag to map port 3000 to our host machine, and `--name` to name it 'my-todo'.
objective: Run the app: `docker run -d -p 3000:3000 --name my-todo todo-app`

title: Updating the Application
description: You found a bug in the code! To deploy a fix, you must first stop and remove the old running container.
objective:
1. Run `docker stop my-todo`
2. Run `docker rm my-todo`

title: Sharing the Application
description: Your Todo App is fixed and ready for production! Let's upload it to Docker Hub so your teammates can pull it and use it.
objective: Push the image to the registry: `docker push todo-app`

title: Persisting the Database
description: When a container is removed, all its data is deleted. To permanently save your Todo list items, we must mount a Docker Volume inside the container.
objective:
1. Run `docker volume create todo-db`
2. Run `docker run -v todo-db:/etc/todos ubuntu`

title: Multi-Container Apps (Networking)
description: Our app is growing. We want to separate the database from the web app. To let containers talk to each other securely, we create custom networks.
objective:
1. Run `docker network create todo-net`
2. Run `docker run --network todo-net todo-app`

title: Docker Compose
description: Typing `docker run`, `network create`, and `volume create` manually is exhausting. Docker Compose orchestrates your entire architecture in one command using a YAML file.
objective: Start the entire multi-container architecture at once: `docker compose up`
hint: Just run `docker compose up` — Compose reads the yaml and starts everything.
why: In production, compose.yaml is the single source of truth for your local dev environment.

title: Multi-Stage Builds
description: A standard Node.js image can be 900MB+. With multi-stage builds, we separate the build environment from the runtime — shrinking images to under 50MB. This reduces attack surface and speeds up deployments.
objective:
1. Build an optimized image: `docker build -t todo-app-slim --target production .`
2. Inspect it: `docker images`
hint: Use `--target` to tell Docker which stage of your multi-stage Dockerfile to stop at.
why: Smaller images deploy faster, use less bandwidth, and expose fewer vulnerabilities — a standard in any production CI/CD pipeline.

title: The Data Loss Scenario
description: Containers are ephemeral — when removed, all internal data is gone forever. Run a database container, add data, then delete it and watch it vanish. This is WHY volumes exist.
objective:
1. Run `docker run --name my-db ubuntu`
2. Remove it: `docker rm my-db`
3. Run `docker inspect my-db` — notice it no longer exists.
hint: After `docker rm`, the container is gone. Any data stored inside it is permanently deleted.
why: Understanding ephemeral state is the #1 aha-moment for Docker beginners. It motivates proper use of Named Volumes in production.

title: Security Hardening
description: By default containers run as root — a serious security risk. If an attacker exploits your app, they have root access to the container. Always run as a non-root user. Also: scan your images for known CVEs.
objective:
1. Run as non-root: `docker run --user 1000 ubuntu`
2. Scan for vulnerabilities: `docker scout ubuntu`
hint: The `--user` flag accepts a UID (like 1000) or a username. Docker Scout is built into the Docker CLI.
why: CIS Docker Benchmark and most enterprise security policies mandate non-root container execution. Shift-Left security means scanning before deployment.

title: Capstone: Production Microservices
description: Bring it all together! Build a production-ready stack: a web service and a database, connected over an isolated network with a persistent volume — then scaffold the whole thing with `docker init`.
objective:
1. Run `docker init` to scaffold the project
2. Create network: `docker network create prod-net`
3. Create volume: `docker volume create prod-db`
4. Start the stack: `docker compose up`
hint: `docker init` auto-generates a Dockerfile, compose.yaml, and .dockerignore for your stack.
why: This is the real-world pattern: network isolation + persistent storage + compose orchestration is the baseline for any production containerized application.
